summary: Leading citizen of Laodicea (inland Phrygian Laodicea, in one of the three Asiatic dioceses attached to Cicero's province of Cilicia); host and friend of Cicero during his proconsulship of Cilicia (51-50 BC), commended to P. Servilius Isauricus in Fam. 13.67. Son of Artemo of Laodicea.
Father of Andro of Laodicea; named only in the patronymic by which Cicero identifies his friend Andro in Fam. 13.67. Distinct from the Sicilian Artemones of the Verrines.

Freedman of C. Rabirius Postumus (taking the nomen Curtius from his patron's adoptive family); a businessman based at Ephesus who hosted Cicero there and managed Cicero's affairs in Asia. Commended to P. Servilius Isauricus in Fam. 13.69 over a property dispute with a man of Colophon.

Roman financier (eques), son of the publican C. Curtius and adopted by his maternal uncle C. Rabirius; a banker on a vast scale entangled in the Egyptian loans to Ptolemy Auletes and defended by Cicero in the Pro Rabirio Postumo (54 BC). Patron of the freedman C. Curtius Mithres and a close friend of Cicero (Fam. 13.69).
An ancient Ionian city of the province of Asia, north of Ephesus. In Fam. 13.69 an unnamed Colophonian is the adversary of Cicero's friend Mithres in a dispute over an estate.
